title: How do I make up for years of missed salah after becoming practicing?
tags: how to make up missed prayers in Islam
do I have to make up years of missed salah
can you make up missed prayers in Islam
stanceSummary: There are two overlapping scholarly opinions on making up years of missed salah. The majority opinion holds that one should make up the missed prayers (qada) by praying extra over the same number of years missed—for example, praying double for 10 years if 10 years were missed, prioritizing qada over sunnah prayers. The minority opinion holds there is no formal qada obligation, but one must still pray extra voluntary prayers as a sign of sincere repentance. Both opinions agree that praying extra is required; the difference is whether the extra prayers are classified as qada or nafl.
alternateQuestions: Do I have to make up years of missed salah?
How to make up missed prayers after becoming practicing Muslim?
Is there a shortcut to make up missed salah like praying at Masjid al-Haram?
What is the ruling on qada salah for someone who wasn't praying?
Can I just repent instead of making up missed prayers?
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

In a [2025 video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mrY7Yqw7eSE) (starting at [0:28](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mrY7Yqw7eSE&t=28s)), Yasir Qadhi addressed the common question of what to do about years of missed salah after becoming practicing. His position:

- **Majority opinion**: You should try to make up all past missed prayers (qada) at a reasonable level — pray extra over roughly the same number of years you missed, prioritizing qada over sunnah prayers.
- **Minority opinion**: There is no formal qada, but you must still pray extra voluntary prayers as a sign of sincere repentance.
- **Both opinions agree** that praying extra is required — the only difference is how those extra prayers are classified.
- There is **no shortcut** such as praying at Masjid al-Haram for the reward of 100,000 prayers to substitute for missed salah.

---

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### The Question — [0:28](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mrY7Yqw7eSE&t=28s)

One of the most common questions we constantly get asked: a person wasn't practicing Islam, started practicing Islam, and they have to make up salah for many, many years. What do they do? Is there something — can we just do one action and get it all done with? Can we go to the Haram and pray one prayer for 100,000? Is there some "buy one, get one free" scheme going on?

### The Two Scholarly Opinions — [0:56](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mrY7Yqw7eSE&t=56s)

This is one of the most common questions asked, and as I have explained multiple times, there are **two opinions**, both of which kind of overlap.

**The first opinion**, which is the dominant majority opinion, is that you should try to make up for all of the past salawat you haven't prayed at a reasonable level. So if you haven't prayed for, let's say, 10 years, try for the next 10 years to do double. That means you prioritize **qada over sunnah** prayers. So instead of praying the sunnah of two rak'ahs before and after, if you haven't prayed for a decade, you pray the fard, and after that you pray another four rak'ahs, and that is the qada for the prayer you haven't done. You do this roughly the same number of years that you weren't praying, and you try to be honest — because in the end, you cannot be exactly sure, but Allah knows you are trying.

**The second opinion** is that there is no formal qada per se, but you still have to pray extra as a sign that you're trying your best to make up. So both opinions say you pray extra. It's just that the first opinion says the extra should be qada, and the second opinion says the extra should be nafl — and Allah is going to forgive me if I'm doing extra.

Without a doubt, this is something that you should try your best to do. Whichever opinion you follow, I don't see that to be a very big deal.
